era_context: Middle Bronze Age. The Old Babylonian period is approaching its end. Egypt is in the Second Intermediate Period with Hyksos presence in the Delta. The Indus Valley Civilization is in its mature phase. Mycenaean Greece is in the Early Bronze Age. This is a transitional period marked by the decline of established powers and the rise of new regional hegemons.

events.title: Old Babylonian dynasty in terminal decline
events.description: Samsu-ditana's reign was in its final years, with the Old Babylonian dynasty facing imminent collapse. The kingdom had lost much of its territorial control and influence. External pressures from the Hittites and other powers, combined with internal instability, were overwhelming the central government. The dynasty would fall within a few years, ending nearly 300 years of Babylonian rule and marking a major transition in Mesopotamian politics.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Samsu-ditana's reign is uncertain, with chronological systems differing by up to 100 years. This year is assigned by convention within the Middle Chronology.

events.title: Hittite consolidation of Anatolian power
events.description: The Hittites under Hantili I were consolidating their control over Anatolia and establishing themselves as a major regional power. This consolidation process involved military campaigns, diplomatic negotiations, and the establishment of administrative structures. The Hittites were beginning to challenge the traditional powers of Mesopotamia and Egypt for regional dominance.

sources.name: Hittite royal inscriptions and later chronicles

events.certainty_note: The specific details of Hittite consolidation in this year are not documented in contemporary sources. The narrative is reconstructed from later Hittite texts and Egyptian records.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization mature phase continuation
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continued to flourish during this period, maintaining its sophisticated urban culture, extensive trade networks, and advanced technology. The civilization was at the height of its development, with major cities functioning as centers of craft production, trade, and administration. The society maintained its distinctive characteristics, including the undeciphered script and standardized weights and measures.
events.certainty_note: The material culture of the Indus Valley Civilization is well-documented through archaeology. The specific dating of this year is based on radiocarbon and stratigraphic analysis.

events.title: Egyptian Second Intermediate Period political fragmentation
events.description: Egypt during the Second Intermediate Period was politically fragmented, with the Hyksos controlling the Delta and Lower Egypt, while native Egyptian dynasties ruled Upper Egypt. This period of division was characterized by competing power centers and a lack of unified central authority. The Hyksos presence was significant and lasting, though the exact nature of their rule and relationship with Egyptian authorities remains debated.
events.certainty_note: The Second Intermediate Period is well-established archaeologically, but contemporary written sources are sparse. Most accounts come from later Egyptian sources written during the New Kingdom.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of this period is heavily debated, with the Low Chronology placing these events approximately 100 years later. The extent and nature of Hittite power consolidation in this specific year are not documented in contemporary sources. The causes of the eventual decline of the Indus Valley Civilization are debated, with multiple factors proposed.
historiographic_note: This year is poorly documented in contemporary written sources. Our knowledge is based on administrative records from Babylon, archaeological evidence from the Indus Valley and Egypt, and later historical compilations. The historical record is fragmentary and heavily dependent on archaeological dating methods with inherent uncertainties. The record is biased toward literate, urban civilizations, and most of the world's population and regions are invisible.

graph_edges.note: The terminal decline of the Old Babylonian dynasty under Samsu-ditana led to its collapse
Hittite consolidation of power led to their emergence as a major regional power
